title: BSU’s Fashion Show returns this Saturday, celebrating of Black Culture through essence and creativity - The New Hampshire
content: University of New Hampshire (UNH) Black Student Union’s (BSU) annual Fashion Show is returning this Saturday, April 18 from 7 p.m. to 9 p.m. in the Memorial Union Building (MUB) Granite State Room (GSR). The BSU executive board expressed the event aims to indulge students in their self-expression through fashion, performance and collaboration while bringing the campus community together in celebration. Each year’s show is centered around themed ‘scenes,’ which remain a surprise until the night of the event. These themes are selected by BSU’s executive board through a collaborative process involving shared ideas, videos and visual inspiration. One previous show, titled “Black to Reality,” offered a futuristic interpretation of Black culture through fashion and art. “We try to let creativity take over,” said BSU Chair Jojo Enelamah. Participants are typically free to choose which scenes they want to join, with many performing in multiple segments. One scene each year is reserved specifically for Black students when the theme centers on Black cultural identity. One of this year’s featured scenes, “Sunday Best,” was inspired by a powerful moment in the film “Sinners,” where a musical performance highlights the evolution of art and culture. Jojo said the scene stood out because it reflects cultural memory and the growth of creative expression over time. “The fashion show is a way for BSU to host an event that channels our creativity and includes our general members and anyone interested in putting themselves out there,” said Enelmah. “It’s a celebration of the time we spend together and something that brings people closer during the school year.” The fashion show has been a tradition for multiple years at UNH, dating back to 2017. Following the COVID-19 pandemic, the BSU executive board has been working to expand the event’s outreach to the UNH community. Third year student Ariyana Greene, a participant in this year’s show, said joining has helped her become more involved on campus. “I wasn’t very involved in my first two years, but this year I wanted to get more connected,” Greene said. “It’s been a great experience. I enjoy seeing all the different roles and the diversity of cultures coming together through creativity.” Third year student Jeremiah Sihotang, who has modeled in the show for the past two years, emphasized the sense of community it creates. “It gives me a sense of comfort and helps me meet new people,” said Sihotang. “We meet twice a week, and you can choose multiple scenes,” he said. “It’s a great way to connect with others and make sure we perform well together.” Layssa Rose, now in her third year participating, said the event has become one of her favorite traditions at UNH. “I wanted to meet new people and do something fun,” said Rose. After serving on the executive board last year, Rose returned as a participant. “Planning was stressful at times, but it came together into a beautiful show,” said Rose. “[BSU Fashion Show] It may take stepping out of your comfort zone,” Enelamah said, “but once you do, it becomes a really fun and rewarding experience.”

title: UNH police investigate reports of man exposing himself on campus - WMUR
content: Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. University of New Hampshire police are investigating reports that a man has been exposing himself to people on campus. UNH police said several people have reached out to them about a man lingering in the area of the campus called College Woods, a popular spot for students to walk or go for a run. According to reports, the man didn't look college-aged and was making people feel uncomfortable. There have been four reports made about the person. Two said the man was in his 30s or 40s and was exposing himself as people passed by. Other reports put the man at about 50 years old and average height. >> Download the free WMUR app to get updates on the go << The latest report said the man was wearing black clothing and a baseball cap and was hanging out in the ravine behind Diamond Library following a student. There was no contact between the two. UNH police said they are alerting students to watch out for anyone acting suspiciously. title: UNH police chief resigns following guilty plea to DUI charge - UnionLeader.com
content:
Isolated thunderstorms this evening followed by a few showers overnight. Low 52F. Winds light and variable. Chance of rain 50%.. Isolated thunderstorms this evening followed by a few showers overnight. Low 52F. Winds light and variable. Chance of rain 50%. Justin Fairfax, a former Virginia lieutenant governor, fatal… About four months ago, three Manchester police officers shot… University of New Hampshire Police Chief Steven Lee has resigned after pleading guilty to driving under the influence following his arrest earlier this year in Portsmouth, university officials confirm. Lee, 50, of Dover, notified UNH Associate General Counsel Tracy Birmingham and Thomas Cronin, UNH chief of staff, of his resignation effective April 1 via email. Lee was notified through his counsel on March 25 that UNH was terminating his employment following an investigation into his arrest on Jan. 22 by Portsmouth Police, according to a copy of a separation agreement between Lee and the school provided to a Union Leader reporter. Lee had been on administrative leave pending an internal investigation since his arrest. Under the terms of the separation agreement, Lee agrees to not apply for or accept another position of employment at UNH or another institution within the University System of New Hampshire (USNH). “Mr. Lee will receive all salary and benefit payments (including payout of earned but unused personal time) to which he is entitled under applicable UNH and University System of New Hampshire policies through the resignation date,” the agreement states. “Mr. Lee agrees and acknowledges that he waives any further right to use the university complaint, appeal or grievance procedures.” Included at the end of the separation agreement as Appendix A is an unsigned letter to Lee accepting his resignation, presumably from a UNH official. “For more than twenty years, you have served the University of New Hampshire with sustained dedication, contributing to the safety and well-being of our community,” the letter says. “The university is grateful for your service. Acknowledging your strong record of service does not diminish the gravity of what occurred earlier this year or the impact of those decisions. “Nevertheless, your actions during this difficult time, including your choice to resign your position, speak highly of your willingness to accept responsibility. On behalf of the University of New Hampshire, I wish you well in the future.” Lee was driving a 2017 black Ford Explorer owned by UNH when he was stopped on the on-ramp for Interstate 95 southbound at Market Street at 12:29 a.m. on Jan. 22 by Portsmouth Police Officer Jayson Page, according to a police narrative obtained by the Union Leader through a Right-to-Know request. In February, Lee pleaded guilty to a charge of DUI. Under the terms of his plea, Lee was ordered to pay a $620 fine, had his driver’s license suspended for nine months (which can be reduced to 90 days) and must take part in a 20-hour Impaired Driver Education Program, court records show. Lee’s attorney, Corey MacDonald, said in a statement his client has “dedicated his career to public safety.” “He took full responsibility for the DUI charge and he pleaded guilty,” MacDonald said. “He did not negotiate the charge down. Many, if not most, first-time DUI offenders have their cases reduced to a reckless driving charge, but Steve wanted to publicly acknowledge that he had made an error in judgement.” According to the police report, Page stopped Lee after observing a UNH-owned SUV “repeatedly going over the white dotted lines into the right lane.” “At one point there was an 18-wheeler in the right lane, the SUV did swerve over the dotted line coming close to colliding with the 18-wheeler,” the police report says. “The SUV then over corrected and swerved back into their lane then going over the yellow solid line.” In the report, Page said he detected “an odor of an alcoholic beverage coming from the vehicle” when speaking with Lee. “Steven stated he was coming from the downtown tonight and was heading home,” the report says. “I asked if he had any drinks while he was out and he stated yes. Steven’s eyes were red and glossy.” Lee partially failed field sobriety tests and declined to take a breathalyzer test, police said. Inside the vehicle police recovered a 9mm Glock, a Taser, riot vest and other equipment. With Lee on paid administrative leave pending a criminal and internal investigation following his arrest, UNH Police Capt. Mark Collopy was appointed interim chief of the department, a role in which he continues to serve, UNH officials said. Collopy has “the confidence of university leadership to ensure continued operations of the department and the safety of the UNH community,” a UNH spokesperson said in an email. Get insights on thousands of stocks from the global community of over 7 million individual investors at Simply Wall St. If you are wondering whether UnitedHealth Group at around US$314 per share is attractively priced or still carrying a premium, you are not alone. The stock has returned 2.6% over the last 7 days and 10.0% over the last 30 days, although the year to date return is a 6.6% decline and the 1 year return is a 44.8% decline. Recent headlines have focused on UnitedHealth Group's role in the wider US healthcare system and ongoing attention on health insurance, cost management and regulatory oversight. This backdrop has kept investors focused on how resilient the business model is and what that might mean for the share price. Simply Wall St currently gives UnitedHealth Group a valuation score of 3 out of 6, which sets up a closer look at how different methods such as multiples and discounted cash flow compare, and how a broader view of valuation at the end of this article can provide an even clearer picture. Find out why UnitedHealth Group's -44.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers. A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value using a required return, giving an estimate of what the business might be worth per share. For UnitedHealth Group, the model used is a 2 stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $15.8b. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations point to projected free cash flow of $27.8b in 2030, with a series of annual projections between 2026 and 2035 that Simply Wall St discounts back to today using its own assumptions. Bringing all those discounted cash flows together produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $816.74 per share. Compared with a current share price around $314, the DCF output suggests the stock trades at a 61.5% discount to this intrinsic value, which indicates that UnitedHealth Group is screened as materially undervalued on this model alone. Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests UnitedHealth Group is undervalued by 61.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks. Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for UnitedHealth Group. For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it ties what you pay per share directly to the earnings that support that price. Investors usually look for a P/E that lines up with the company’s growth outlook and perceived risk, with higher growth or lower risk often justifying a higher multiple. UnitedHealth Group currently trades on a P/E of 23.6x. That sits above the Healthcare industry average of 22.8x and above the peer average of 20.3x. On these simple comparisons, the shares are priced at a higher earnings multiple than many peers. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for UnitedHealth Group is 36.3x. This is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might reasonably be, given factors such as earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market capitalization and company specific risks. Because it pulls these elements together in one metric, the Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight comparison with peer or industry averages, which do not fully reflect differences in growth or risk. With the Fair Ratio above the current 23.6x P/E, this approach suggests the shares are trading below what that earnings based framework would indicate. P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies. Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so here is Narratives, a way for you to put a clear story behind the numbers you see for UnitedHealth Group. A Narrative is simply your view of how a company works, written into the assumptions behind fair value, revenue, earnings and margin estimates, so that the story and the spreadsheet line up instead of sitting apart. On Simply Wall St, Narratives are available on the Community page and used by millions of investors as an accessible tool that links a company’s story to a forecast and then to an implied fair value. You can then compare that implied fair value with the current share price to decide whether you see a potential opportunity or risk. These Narratives update automatically when new earnings, news or guidance are fed in, so your story and valuation stay current rather than frozen at the time you first ran the numbers. For UnitedHealth Group, one investor Narrative might anchor on a fair value around US$198 with more conservative assumptions about Medicare pressure. Another might point to a fair value near US$626 with a more optimistic view on margins and long term earnings. Seeing those side by side helps you decide which story, numbers and risk trade off feel more realistic for you. For UnitedHealth Group however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading UnitedHealth Group Narratives: Start by comparing these against your own view of the business, then decide which set of assumptions feels closer to how you think things play out. Current price vs this fair value: around 13.8% below that estimate of fair value. Analysts building this Narrative see UnitedHealth Group working through Medicare and CMS related challenges over time, with technology investment and value based care programs intended to support a gradual earnings recovery. The view assumes revenue growing to an expected US$501.1b and earnings of US$20.0b by around 2028, with a P/E of 17.0x on those earnings and slightly lower profit margins than today. This Narrative aligns with a consensus price target of US$327.29, with a wide range of views between US$198.00 and US$626.00 that reflects disagreement on how quickly earnings and margins might settle. Current price vs this fair value: around 1.9% above that estimate of fair value. This Narrative focuses on UnitedHealth Group as a large, integrated healthcare platform where scale, data and Optum integration matter, but where the share price can be sensitive to regulatory pressure and questions over how that integration affects competition. The author highlights that while diversification across insurance, pharmacy, analytics and care delivery provides resilience, it also attracts regulatory and political attention that could weigh on valuation if sentiment or policy turns less friendly. Here the emphasis is on the risk of valuation multiples compressing if growth at Optum slows or if investors become more cautious about margins, even if the underlying business model remains embedded in the US healthcare system. title: Virtual reality headsets ‘bring outer space closer to home’ for N.H. third-graders - The Boston Globe
content: DOVER, N.H. — Third-graders at Garrison Elementary School learning all about astronomy this month got to experience something a bit more immersive than a traditional textbook. A team of space scientists from the University of New Hampshire visited their classroom on Tuesday, when the students took turns trying on virtual reality headsets and stepping into a digital laboratory that mimics the physics of space. Charlie Brennan, a third-grader who wore a sweatshirt celebrating the popular computer game Minecraft, smiled big as he donned the VR headset and explored the three-dimensional sandbox environment. title: DUI conviction leads to resignation of UNH police chief - Valley News
content:
You can now receive the latest newsletter from the Valley News directly in your inbox and never miss a headline. Sign up for the Valley News' morning newsletter and get essential news each day. University of New Hampshire Police Chief Steven Lee has resigned two months after being arrested in Portsmouth, N.H., for driving under the influence. Lee’s resignation was effective April 1, according to Tania deLuzuriaga, a university spokesperson. His decision to resign came after the university notified him it intended to terminate his employment, according to a separation agreement obtained through a right-to-know request. “He believes that his transition from the university is in the Department’s best interest given the unfortunate event that occurred,” Lee’s attorney, Corey MacDonald, wrote in a statement to the Monitor. Lee, 50, was arrested in the early morning hours of Jan. 22 after an officer spotted his SUV swerving and nearly colliding with an 18-wheeler on Market Street in Portsmouth, according to a police report the Monitor obtained through a right-to-know request. Lee admitted to drinking “downtown” and said he was heading home, according to the report. The officer said Lee smelled of alcohol and noticed he had red, glossy eyes. He partially failed a series of field sobriety tests and declined to take a breathalyzer test. Award-winning coverage on breaking news and today’s top stories in and around the Upper Valley. Whether it's arts, sports, opinion, world news,  or our archives and e-editions, you’ll have unlimited access to the Valley News' content. He had been on administrative leave since his arrest. In February, he pleaded guilty to the DUI charge. “Steve has taken the high road at every opportunity throughout this unfortunate process, insisting on full accountability and transparency,” MacDonald wrote. “He has acknowledged his mistake, and he has faced consequences that go far and away beyond what usually occurs when there is a DUI conviction.” Lee was named UNH’s police chief last year, following the departure of his predecessor, Paul Dean, who took a job in Gov. Kelly Ayotte’s administration. Lee worked full-time for the department for nearly two decades. As a condition of his plea, Lee’s driver’s license was suspended for nine months, with the possibility of earlier reinstatement. He was required to pay a $620 fine and participate in an impaired driver care management program, according to court records. MacDonald previously said that Lee placed himself on administrative leave and “proactively entered treatment catering to first responders.” “He is hopeful as he looks forward to the next chapter of his professional life, and he considers himself fortunate to have spent 30 years in public service doing good for his community,” MacDonald wrote. “Steve encourages other first responders to not take their own health and well-being lightly, and to lean into each other for guidance and support early on in their career.” In a letter accepting Lee’s resignation that was appended to his separation agreement, an unnamed university official thanked Lee for his “sustained dedication” to the university, but noted that it “does not diminish the gravity of what occurred earlier this year.” Former Captain Mark Collopy was appointed interim chief of UNH’s department following Lee’s arrest. He continues to serve in that role, according to deLuzuriaga. The university has yet to launch a search to fill the role permanently. The chief of police reports to the university’s vice president for administration and finance, Robert Watling, who started in that position within the past month, deLuzuriaga said. Jeremy Margolis is the Monitor's education reporter. He also covers the towns of Boscawen, Salisbury, and Webster, and the courts. Analysts on Wall Street project that UnitedHealth Group (UNH) will announce quarterly earnings of $6.48 per share in its forthcoming report, representing a decline of 10% year over year. Revenues are projected to reach $109.45 billion, declining 0.1% from the same quarter last year. The consensus EPS estimate for the quarter has been revised 4.6% higher over the last 30 days to the current level. This reflects how the analysts covering the stock have collectively reevaluated their initial estimates during this timeframe. Before a company announces its earnings, it is essential to take into account any changes made to earnings estimates. This is a valuable factor in predicting the potential reactions of investors toward the stock. Empirical research has consistently shown a strong correlation between trends in earnings estimate revisions and the short-term price performance of a stock. While investors typically use consensus earnings and revenue estimates as a yardstick to evaluate the company's quarterly performance, scrutinizing analysts' projections for some of the company's key metrics can offer a more comprehensive perspective. With that in mind, let's delve into the average projections of some UnitedHealth metrics that are commonly tracked and projected by analysts on Wall Street. Analysts predict that the 'Revenues- Investment and other income' will reach $977.63 million. The estimate indicates a year-over-year change of -5.4%. title: UNH Stock Is Down 46% — But Is the Worst Finally Over for UnitedHealth? - ABC Money
content:

A particular type of stock is able to overcome the burden of its own reputation. Over the past 12 months, UNH stock has been that kind of story—one of those situations where everything that could go wrong did, frequently in public and frequently painfully, but the business continued to make billions of dollars in cash underneath all the commotion. A portion of the story is revealed by the numbers. UnitedHealth Group’s stock dropped from a 52-week high of $594.81 to about $314, a 46.5% decline over the previous year. It’s not a correction. For a business that was recently regarded as one of the most stable and reliable brands in the Dow Jones Industrial Average, that is more akin to a reckoning. Due to the nearly simultaneous arrival of the list of issues, it is difficult to summarize what caused the slide. The biggest health payments platform in the nation was shut down for months due to the Change Healthcare ransomware attack in early 2024, which cost hundreds of millions of dollars in recovery and ransom. Medicare billing practices were the subject of Justice Department investigations. Investors used to UnitedHealth’s consistency were alarmed when the medical loss ratio, which measures the percentage of premium revenue that is actually paid out in claims, increased to 88.9% in 2025. The CEO of UnitedHealthcare’s insurance division, Brian Thompson, was shot and killed on December 4, 2024, outside a Manhattan hotel where the company was holding an investor event. The social media backlash was unsettling not only because of its intensity but also because it showed how much the general public had grown resentful of the health insurance sector. Something seemed to change at that moment, though it’s still unclear exactly what. In the midst of all of this, UNH stock has begun to garner new interest. UnitedHealth is one of the most textbook examples the framework has produced in years, according to strategists who adhere to the Dogs of the Dow, the traditional contrarian income strategy of purchasing the highest-yielding Dow components each year. The reasoning is well-known: when the stock of a reputable blue-chip company declines sufficiently, the dividend yield increases, and the price itself becomes a possible source of return once conditions stabilize. UnitedHealth plans to pay out about $8 billion in dividends and $2.5 billion in share repurchases in 2026 alone. At current prices, the company’s annualized dividend of $8.84 per share yields about 2.8%. Following Andrew Witty’s resignation, CEO Stephen Hemsley returned to the top position and has framed the company’s trajectory with a cautious confidence that suggests management is aware that the upcoming quarters will be closely monitored. Following the fourth-quarter results, he stated, “We confronted challenges directly and finished 2025 as a much stronger company.” His words were measured, perhaps even defensive, but not dismissive. Some optimism is supported by his 2026 guidance, which calls for operating earnings above $24 billion and adjusted earnings per share above $17.75, up from $16.35 in 2025. There are indications that the insiders themselves believe the stock is inexpensive. Ten board directors purchased shares at the same time on April 1, 2026; this was a coordinated action by all of them rather than a token purchase from a single executive. On March 17, the CEOs of UnitedHealthcare, Optum, and CFO all added shares. The people closest to the company’s books may believe that the market has overshot on pessimism, but that kind of insider activity does not guarantee anything. However, the dangers are not insignificant. UnitedHealthcare’s enrollment is predicted to decline from 49.8 million to between 46.9 and 47.5 million in 2026. This significant drop raises concerns about whether the company has fully recovered from the coverage pullbacks that impacted margins or if they are still making their way through the system. The predicted improvement in the medical care ratio for 2026, which is only a tenth of a percentage point, is so nearly flat that it is only a stabilization rather than a true recovery signal. Furthermore, it is challenging to price the Justice Department’s continued interest in the business’s Medicare practices into a forward estimate. The current state of UNH stock makes seasoned investors uneasy in two different ways at once: it is inexpensive enough to feel like a chance, but it is also complex enough to feel like a trap. As of mid-April 2026, a Polymarket prediction market gave the stock a 57.5% chance of exceeding its upcoming quarterly earnings, which is a slight advantage but not a strong recommendation. Although consensus targets have previously been incorrect on this name, Wall Street’s consensus of 23 buy ratings against just one sell indicates sincere belief in the turnaround thesis. There is more to UnitedHealth than just a single stock call. It operates Optum, its pharmacy and health services division, as a Fortune 500 company on its own, and is the largest health insurer in the nation, providing coverage to over 50 million people through UnitedHealthcare. Questions about pricing power, regulatory exposure, and whether the vertically integrated model that made UnitedHealth so dominant for so long still operates as it once did are raised when a company this deeply ingrained in the American healthcare system experiences turbulence of this magnitude. Investors appear to think that eventually the answer is yes. The question of whether it will eventually arrive in 2026 or take longer remains unanswered. title: UNH Cheer Repeats as NCA Advanced All-Girl National Champions - University of New Hampshire Athletics
content:
DAYTONA BEACH, Fla. - The UNH cheerleading program delivered standout performances on the national stage, winning the National Cheerleading Association (NCA) Advanced All-Girl Division I National Championship for the fourth time while also earning the All-Girl Grand Championship Title. The Wildcats also finished third in the Advanced Small Coed Division I at the NCA College Nationals held April 8-11, 2026, at the historic Daytona Beach Bandshell. Considered the premier event in collegiate cheerleading, the NCA College Nationals featured hundreds of teams from across the country competing for championship honors. UNH's 38 students competed in both the Advanced All-Girl Division I and Advanced Small Coed Division I, producing strong, disciplined routines in each division – without any deductions – and continuing the program's tradition of excellence. 

CLICK HERE TO SEE FULL RESULTS The Wildcats' Advanced All-Girl Division I team earned the national title with a final score of 96.675, finishing ahead of California Baptist University (95.4792) and Utah Valley University (94.0583). UNH delivered a difficult, high-energy routine to secure the program's fourth NCA Advanced All-Girl national championship for the second consecutive year. In addition to winning the division title, UNH captured All-Girl Grand Championship honors, posting a score of 94.9 in the finals to outpace Oklahoma Baptist University (94.6333) and the University of Louisville (93.95). The Grand Championship recognizes the top overall All-Girl performance across divisions at the NCA College Nationals. This latest championship continues the program's tradition of success under the leadership of head coach Scott Rigoli and assistant coach Brad Burlamachi. UNH's All-Girl Division I titles now span 2017, 2023, 2025, and 2026, with the 2023 team also earning the NCA All-Girl Grand Championship. The Advanced Small Coed Division I team, in only its second year of existence, closed out the weekend with a third-place national finish, scoring 89.375 in a highly competitive field. Weber State University claimed the division title with a 96.0125, followed by the University of Central Arkansas (91.8792). "Coming home from Daytona, I couldn't be prouder of how our athletes represented UNH on an incredibly competitive national stage," Rigoli said. "The All-Girl team rose to the moment and delivered their strongest performances at the right time, earning both the national championship and Grand Championship. Our Small Coed team showed tremendous growth, competing with confidence and grit against elite programs. This group continues to set a high standard for what UNH cheerleading is all about which helps us shine a bright light on the University and attract the very best talent." "I am extremely proud of our UNH Cheerleading program for another historic showing at the NCA College Nationals," said Allison Rich, Director of Athletics. "Winning a fourth Advanced All-Girl Division I national championship and capturing the All-Girl Grand Championship is a remarkable achievement and speaks to the dedication of our students and coaching staff. UNH Cheerleading consistently represents our university with excellence and pride." ABOUT UNH CHEERLEADING 
UNH Cheerleading brings Wildcat spirit to select athletic contests by entertaining the students and fans at athletic venues and community events. The team provides spirit and entertainment at home football games, men's and women's basketball games, and select away/postseason athletic events.  In addition to promoting various athletic events on campus, the team trains and practices year-round to compete locally and nationally. They compete annually at NCA College Nationals in Daytona Beach, Fla. The cheerleading program is supported and partially funded by the UNH Department of Athletics. The team also participates in various fundraising events throughout the year to help fund the cost of travel, entry fees, and hotel accommodations. 
  
If you'd like to donate to the team, PLEASE CLICK HERE.

title: Anti-War Rally Held on UNH’s Thompson Hall Lawn - The New Hampshire
content: On April 14, outside of Thompson Hall on the University of New Hampshire (UNH) campus, students and community members gathered to protest the ongoing war in Iran. The event was organized by the student organization UNH Young Americans for Liberty (YAL), in collaboration with UNH College Republicans and the UNH Turning Point USA chapter. “Young Americans for Liberty has always been a very anti-war organization,” said Matt Brooks, one of the event organizers and an executive board member of UNH YAL. Students holding protest signs and flags Credit: Rebecca Colby The Iran war began on Feb. 28, when the United States and Israel launched targeted strikes across Iran, one of which killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the nation’s supreme leader. Since then, various airstrikes and bombings have been carried out, resulting in numerous fatalities on both sides. The United States and Iran are currently in ceasefire discussions; however, tensions remain high. “I hosted [this event]to really make it clear that students care about these issues,” said Alexander Fenton, the president of UNH YAL and the event’s organizer. “The whole point is that we’re anti-war and we should come together as students.” The rally was sponsored in part by Bruce Fenton, a Durham resident and father of the YAL chapter president. “I like to encourage anything that’s an anti-war, pro-peace message with UNH because it's close to the heart, being a New Hampshire local resident,” said Fenton. Fenton says he has always been passionate about anti-war causes, and was active in the anti-war movement during the Gulf War and conflicts in Afghanistan. Rep. Kelley Potenza (R-N.H.) of  Strafford District 19, attended and spoke at the event. “I just can’t stay silent. So many people voted for an administration, a president that repeatedly said ‘no more, no more foreign endless wars, no more—all of this’, and here we are in another conflict.” Several members of the Palestinian Support Coalition (PSC) were in attendance, including founding member and president Emory Stevens, a third-year UNH student. “We’re here with PSC in observance of this event and also to try and let people know the true nature of this event, who it's being hosted by, and their real reasons for being out here,” said Stevens. PSC Members holding the Palestinian flag  Credit: Rebecca Colby Stevens noted that the rally focused primarily on United States soldiers entering into combat in Iran, instead of the civilians living in those areas. “Of course it is horrible, but it is about the Iranian people, it is about the Palestinian people, it’s about the Lebanese people. That is what we need to be worried about, and we need to focus on the protection and safety of those people, ” said Stevens.

title: UnitedHealth vs. Elevance: Which Healthcare Stock Has More Upside? - The Globe and Mail
content: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated UNH and Elevance Health, Inc. ELV are two of the largest players in the U.S. managed care space, operating at a time when rising medical costs, shifting reimbursement structures and growing demand for integrated care are redefining industry dynamics. Both companies offer diversified exposure across insurance and healthcare services, making them central to how the sector adapts to cost pressures and evolving care models. Despite these similarities, their strategic positioning differs in meaningful ways. UnitedHealth has built a deeply integrated ecosystem through its Optum platform, emphasizing care delivery and data-led services, while Elevance remains more anchored in its core insurance business with a gradual expansion into services. These differences influence how each company approaches growth, cost management and earnings diversification in a changing healthcare landscape. Also, improving policy visibility is acting as a tailwind, with the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services finalizing Medicare Advantage payment rates for 2027 at an average increase of 2.48%, significantly higher than the earlier 0.09% proposal in January, thereby strengthening sector sentiment and revenue outlook. Let’s dive deep and closely compare the fundamentals to determine which stock offers greater upside right now. UnitedHealth’s strength lies in its deeply integrated model, where insurance and healthcare services operate in tandem. Through its Optum segment, the company has built a high-margin services engine that spans pharmacy benefit management, care delivery and data analytics. This vertical integration not only enhances profitability but also creates a buffer against rising medical costs, allowing UnitedHealth to maintain earnings stability even when insurance margins tighten. The company’s total revenues rose 12.3% year over year in the fourth quarter of 2025, with 17.5% growth at UnitedHealthcare and 8% growth at Optum. A key strategic shift is the company’s willingness to accept membership contraction to restore margins. UnitedHealth is guiding for a notable decline in Medicare Advantage and overall membership in 2026, driven by deliberate product repositioning and pricing actions. This signals a pivot toward profitability and sustainability over short-term enrollment gains. It expects medical memberships to be in the range of 46.945-47.495 million in 2026, with significant declines in commercial risk and Medicaid heads. UnitedHealth’s scale provides a significant competitive advantage. Its ability to negotiate provider rates, invest in technology and expand care delivery infrastructure reinforces its leadership position. While membership growth may moderate in the near term, the company’s earnings power remains anchored in its diversified revenue streams and operational efficiency, making it a relatively defensive play in the sector. Financially, UNH is in a solid position. It ended 2025 with $28.1 billion in cash and short-term investments, sufficient to cover its short-term borrowings and current maturities of long-term debt, which stands at $6.1 billion. Its total debt-to-capital of 41.95% is below ELV’s 42.01% and the industry’s 44.04%. The company also rewarded more than $13 billion to its shareholders in the form of share repurchases and dividends during 2025. UNH missed earnings estimates twice in the past four quarters and beat on the other occasions. UnitedHealth Group Incorporated price-consensus-eps-surprise-chart | UnitedHealth Group Incorporated Quote Elevance’s positioning is supported by a diversified healthcare platform and a strategy centered on improving care affordability and outcomes. The company continues to build capabilities around whole-person health, integrating medical, pharmacy and behavioral services to enhance care coordination and member experience. This approach is gaining traction with employers and government programs, reinforcing its relevance in an increasingly complex healthcare landscape. The company is actively reshaping its portfolio with a clear focus on sustainability. Across Medicare Advantage, Medicaid and ACA markets, Elevance is taking deliberate actions to refine its product mix, emphasizing segments that align with long-term margin and performance objectives. This repositioning reflects a measured approach to reducing volatility and improving the consistency of earnings. Another key aspect of Elevance’s strategy is its ongoing transformation into a broader healthcare platform. Through its Carelon segment, the company is investing in services such as pharmacy, behavioral health and care management. Although still in a scaling phase compared to UNH’s Optum, Carelon represents a strategic pivot toward higher-value, service-driven revenue streams that could enhance profitability over time. ELV’s total revenues rose 9.5% year over year in the fourth quarter of 2025, along with 27% growth in operating revenues for Carelon. It beat earnings estimates in three of the past four quarters and missed once, with an average surprise of 7.9%. Elevance Health, Inc. price-consensus-eps-surprise-chart | Elevance Health, Inc. Quote ELV exited the fourth quarter of 2025 with a solid liquidity position, holding $9.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents. The company also demonstrates relatively stronger capital efficiency, with a return on invested capital of 8.3%, notably higher than UnitedHealth at 5.4%. In 2025, Elevance returned $4.1 billion to its shareholders through repurchases and dividends. Its dividend yield of 2.2% is lower than that of UNH’s 2.8%. The Zacks Consensus Estimate favors UNH at this stage. The consensus estimate for UNH’s 2026 earnings indicates an 8% increase from a year ago. Meanwhile, the consensus estimate for revenues suggests a 1.6% decline. On the other hand, the consensus estimate for ELV’s 2026 earnings indicates a 14.4% decline from a year ago, while the same for revenues suggests a 1.4% decline. Coming to the valuation story, it seems that investors are willing to pay a premium for UnitedHealth compared to Elevance. This is reflected in UNH’s forward 12-month price/earnings (P/E) of 17.16X compared with ELV’s 11.66X. Both are currently trading below their five-year median P/E value. In the year-to-date period, UnitedHealth has shed less value than Elevance. However, the S&P 500 has increased 1.9% during this time. UnitedHealth and Elevance are both navigating a complex healthcare environment marked by elevated cost trends and shifting policy dynamics. While ELV is focused on execution, portfolio refinement and building out its Carelon platform, its near term outlook remains constrained by margin pressure in Medicaid and a reset in earnings expectations. In contrast, UNH appears better positioned to deliver more stable and durable growth, supported by its scale, diversified revenue streams and the strength of its Optum platform. Its ability to balance margin recovery with operational efficiency and capital strength provides greater visibility into earnings. As a result, UNH currently stands out as the relatively stronger play for investors seeking resilience and long-term upside in the managed care space. While UnitedHealth currently carries a Zacks Rank #3 (Hold), Elevance has a Zacks Rank #4 (Sell). You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank (Strong Buy) stocks here. While not all picks can be winners, previous recommendations have soared +112%, +171%, +209% and +232%. Through its Optum segment, the company has built a high-margin services engine that spans pharmacy benefit management, care delivery and data analytics. This vertical integration not only enhances profitability but also creates a buffer against rising medical costs, allowing UnitedHealth to maintain earnings stability even when insurance margins tighten. The company’s total revenues rose 12.3% year over year in the fourth quarter of 2025, with 17.5% growth at UnitedHealthcare and 8% growth at Optum. A key strategic shift is the company’s willingness to accept membership contraction to restore margins. UnitedHealth is guiding for a notable decline in Medicare Advantage and overall membership in 2026, driven by deliberate product repositioning and pricing actions. This signals a pivot toward profitability and sustainability over short-term enrollment gains. It expects medical memberships to be in the range of 46.945-47.495 million in 2026, with significant declines in commercial risk and Medicaid heads. UnitedHealth’s scale provides a significant competitive advantage. Its ability to negotiate provider rates, invest in technology and expand care delivery infrastructure reinforces its leadership position. While membership growth may moderate in the near term, the company’s earnings power remains anchored in its diversified revenue streams and operational efficiency, making it a relatively defensive play in the sector. Financially, UNH is in a solid position. It ended 2025 with $28.1 billion in cash and short-term investments, sufficient to cover its short-term borrowings and current maturities of long-term debt, which stands at $6.1 billion. Its total debt-to-capital of 41.95% is below ELV’s 42.01% and the industry’s 44.04%. The company also rewarded more than $13 billion to its shareholders in the form of share repurchases and dividends during 2025. UNH missed earnings estimates twice in the past four quarters and beat on the other occasions. UnitedHealth Group Incorporated Price, Consensus and EPS Surprise UnitedHealth Group Incorporated price-consensus-eps-surprise-chart | UnitedHealth Group Incorporated Quote Elevance’s positioning is supported by a diversified healthcare platform and a strategy centered on improving care affordability and outcomes. The company continues to build capabilities around whole-person health, integrating medical, pharmacy and behavioral services to enhance care coordination and member experience. This approach is gaining traction with employers and government programs, reinforcing its relevance in an increasingly complex healthcare landscape. The company is actively reshaping its portfolio with a clear focus on sustainability. Across Medicare Advantage, Medicaid and ACA markets, Elevance is taking deliberate actions to refine its product mix, emphasizing segments that align with long-term margin and performance objectives. This repositioning reflects a measured approach to reducing volatility and improving the consistency of earnings. Another key aspect of Elevance’s strategy is its ongoing transformation into a broader healthcare platform. Through its Carelon segment, the company is investing in services such as pharmacy, behavioral health and care management. Although still in a scaling phase compared to UNH’s Optum, Carelon represents a strategic pivot toward higher-value, service-driven revenue streams that could enhance profitability over time. ELV’s total revenues rose 9.5% year over year in the fourth quarter of 2025, along with 27% growth in operating revenues for Carelon. It beat earnings estimates in three of the past four quarters and missed once, with an average surprise of 7.9%. Elevance Health, Inc. Quote ELV exited the fourth quarter of 2025 with a solid liquidity position, holding $9.5 billion in cash and cash equivalents. The company also demonstrates relatively stronger capital efficiency, with a return on invested capital of 8.3%, notably higher than UnitedHealth at 5.4%. In 2025, Elevance returned $4.1 billion to its shareholders through repurchases and dividends. Its dividend yield of 2.2% is lower than that of UNH’s 2.8%. The Zacks Consensus Estimate favors UNH at this stage. The consensus estimate for UNH’s 2026 earnings indicates an 8% increase from a year ago. Meanwhile, the consensus estimate for revenues suggests a 1.6% decline. On the other hand, the consensus estimate for ELV’s 2026 earnings indicates a 14.4% decline from a year ago, while the same for revenues suggests a 1.4% decline. Coming to the valuation story, it seems that investors are willing to pay a premium for UnitedHealth compared to Elevance. This is reflected in UNH’s forward 12-month price/earnings (P/E) of 17.16X compared with ELV’s 11.66X. Both are currently trading below their five-year median P/E value. In the year-to-date period, UnitedHealth has shed less value than Elevance. However, the S&P 500 has increased 1.9% during this time. UnitedHealth and Elevance are both navigating a complex healthcare environment marked by elevated cost trends and shifting policy dynamics. While ELV is focused on execution, portfolio refinement and building out its Carelon platform, its near term outlook remains constrained by margin pressure in Medicaid and a reset in earnings expectations. In contrast, UNH appears better positioned to deliver more stable and durable growth, supported by its scale, diversified revenue streams and the strength of its Optum platform. Its ability to balance margin recovery with operational efficiency and capital strength provides greater visibility into earnings. As a result, UNH currently stands out as the relatively stronger play for investors seeking resilience and long-term upside in the managed care space. title: The Giant Realigns: A 2026 Deep Dive into UnitedHealth Group (UNH) - FinancialContent
content:
The healthcare landscape of 2026 stands at a crossroads, and no company embodies this transition more than UnitedHealth Group (NYSE: UNH). Once the undisputed "gold standard" for consistent double-digit earnings growth, the Minnetonka-based giant has spent the last 24 months navigating a gauntlet of operational crises, regulatory shifts, and a fundamental valuation reset. Today, as of April 15, 2026, investors are closely watching UNH as it executes a massive "right-sizing" strategy aimed at restoring the stability and profitability that defined it for decades. UnitedHealth Group remains the largest healthcare organization in the world by revenue, a massive enterprise that touches nearly every aspect of the American medical system. From providing insurance to over 50 million people to managing the prescriptions of 100 million more through its OptumRx division, UNH is often viewed as a proxy for the U.S. healthcare economy. However, the narrative around the stock has shifted from "growth at any cost" to "resilience and recovery." Following a volatile 2025, which saw the company suspend guidance for the first time in years due to surging medical costs, UNH enters the second quarter of 2026 with a leaner portfolio and a renewed focus on its "back-to-basics" clinical model. Founded in 1977 by Richard Burke as United HealthCare Corporation, the company went public in 1984. Its early years were defined by pioneering the Health Maintenance Organization (HMO) model, but its true transformation began in the early 2000s under the leadership of Bill McGuire and later Stephen Hemsley. The pivotal moment came in 2011 with the creation of Optum, which separated health services from health insurance. This "Payvider" model—where the company both pays for care and provides it—allowed UNH to capture margins across the entire healthcare delivery chain. Over the years, strategic acquisitions like Catamaran (PBM) and DaVita Medical Group solidified its dominance. However, 2024 and 2025 proved to be the most challenging years in its history, marked by the massive Change Healthcare cyberattack and the tragic loss of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson in December 2024. As of mid-April 2026, UNH is trading in the $315 range, a significant departure from its all-time highs of 2024. The fiscal year 2025 was a "kitchen sink" year for UNH. The company reported revenue of $447.6 billion but saw its adjusted earnings per share (EPS) drop to $16.35 as its Medical Care Ratio (MCR) spiked to an alarming 89.1%.
For 2026, management has issued a "rebound" outlook. They are intentionally shrinking revenue to approximately $439 billion by exiting underperforming Medicare Advantage markets and non-core international assets like Banmédica in South America. The goal is to return to an adjusted EPS of $17.75+ by focusing on margin expansion over sheer volume. Debt-to-total-capitalization remains healthy at approximately 38%, and cash flow from operations is expected to hit $24 billion this year. In a move that surprised Wall Street in May 2025, former CEO Stephen J. Hemsley returned to the helm following the resignation of Andrew Witty. Hemsley’s return was signaled as a "stabilizing" move to restore operational discipline. He is joined by CFO Wayne S. DeVeydt, a veteran healthcare executive formerly of Elevance Health, who was hired to rein in medical costs. Dr. Patrick Conway leads Optum, focusing on the clinical integration of AI, while Tim Noel manages the UnitedHealthcare insurance portfolio. The team’s primary mandate in 2026 is to repair the company’s reputation with providers and regulators following the 2024 cyberattack. Innovation at UNH in 2026 is centered on "Agentic AI" and clinical automation. UNH faces stiff competition from other vertically integrated giants: The "Silver Tsunami"—the aging of the U.S. population—remains the most significant tailwind for the sector. However, the trend of medical cost inflation (driven by higher utilization and the cost of new GLP-1 weight-loss drugs) has become a major headwind. Additionally, there is a macro shift toward "transparency" in drug pricing, which is forcing a redesign of the traditional PBM business model. Wall Street sentiment is currently "Cautiously Bullish." While 2025 was a disaster for the share price, many analysts believe the bottom is in. Institutional investors, including BlackRock and Vanguard, have maintained their core positions, viewing the 2025 crash as a rare opportunity to buy a "generational compounder" at a discount. Retail chatter remains mixed, with some concerns over the cultural fallout of the Thompson tragedy and the company's aggressive prior-authorization practices. The regulatory environment is the most hostile it has been in a decade. Beyond PBM reform, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) have continued to tighten reimbursement rates. Geopolitically, UNH’s decision to exit the South American market (selling Banmédica) reflects a strategic pivot to focus purely on the U.S. regulatory and clinical landscape, reducing its exposure to foreign currency and political risk. UnitedHealth Group in 2026 is a company in the midst of a profound transformation. The "Payvider" model is being tested by new laws and a surge in healthcare demand. However, with the return of Stephen Hemsley and a ruthless focus on clinical efficiency, UNH is attempting to prove that its scale is an asset rather than a liability. For investors, the current valuation represents a bet on whether the "right-sizing" of 2026 can pave the way for a more sustainable, if perhaps slower, growth trajectory in the years to come. Watch the Q3 2026 Medical Care Ratio carefully—it will be the definitive signal of whether the giant has truly regained its footing. title: Campus carry clash: Students and police warn of chaos, supporters cite rights - UnionLeader.com
content: Personal liberty squared off against concern over higher education safety during what may have been the final public hearing Tuesday on sweeping legislation (HB 1793) to allow students, staff and visitors to carry weapons on all public college campuses in the state. State Rep. Samuel Farrington, R-Rochester, a University of New Hampshire student who sponsored the bill, faced a flurry of questions from Republican senators on the Senate Judiciary Committee who questioned whether the bill was too loosely drawn. Farrington said he’d be open to amendments to ensure guns inside a dorm were placed inside a locked box and that colleges clearly had the authority to criminally punish anyone who was impaired on campus and was carrying a weapon. At the same time, Farrington said the principle of letting students possess guns anywhere so they can defend themselves was to him non-negotiable. “Right now, they are stripped — adults, students, faculty, professors — of their natural rights,” Farrington said. “Individuals have a natural right to defend themselves with a firearm.” The presidents of the three public colleges at Durham, Keene and Plymouth, along with their campus police executives, warned the policy could lead to more violence, suicide attempts and a chaotic scene if shots are fired. “This is not about (gun) rights; this is about common sense,” UNH Deputy Police Chief Jack Dalton said at a rally outside the State House before the hearing. “Now we could have 14-15 people running in different directions, all of them with weapons and people shouting, ‘That’s the shooter.’” UNH President Elizabeth Chilton urged the Senate to send the bill off for more study. “New Hampshire has some of the safest college campuses in the country and there is no evidence that this bill will make it any safer,” Chilton said. Rep. Robert Lynn, R-Windham, a retired Supreme Court chief justice, said gun control advocates warned of New Hampshire becoming the “Wild, Wild West” when the Republican-led Legislature in 2017 adopted constitutional carry that allows legally eligible adults to possess a concealed weapon. “All those fears proved to be unfounded back then and I suspect they would be again if we pass this,” Lynn said. Eli Orne, a UNH freshman studying to be a clinical psychologist, said allowing students to have loaded guns would put many young people at risk of harming themselves. “I’ve known so many students, including myself, who have gone through mental health struggles transitioning from high school to the college environment,” Orne said. Committee Chairman Bill Gannon, R-Sandown, and Vice Chairman Daryl Abbas, R-Salem, test-marketed potential amendments such as preventing open carry by a student in class, restricting gun carrying inside dorms and adopting stiff penalties for anyone drunk with a gun on campus. “Can you tell me which amendments you could support?” Abbas asked more than one witness during several hours of testimony. Rep. Valerie McDonnell, R-Salem, said she preferred the original bill, but would not oppose the Senate adopting “time, place and manner restrictions” regarding dangerous weapon possession everywhere on school grounds. Penny Dean, a Second Amendment advocate and lawyer, said she believed allowing women students to possess guns would reduce the incidence of rape on campuses. A UNH Student Senate survey of 2,300 students found 81% strongly opposed the bill. A UNH Survey Center poll of residents statewide was more split with 49% against it, 35% for it, 13% neutral and the other 4% with no opinion. At the rally, more than 100 opponents held signs with messages including “Kids should be sitting at desks, not hiding under them,” “Colleges are places of learning, not guns,” and “Hold my beer. Campus Carry. Really?” John McKenna with the national Campaign to Keep Guns Off Campus said the House-passed bill would be the most open-ended of any in the nation in part because it would include a $10,000 fine for every violation. John Bunker, a former UNH associate dean and New Hampshire board member of the National Alliance on Mental Illness, said a 2025 study paid for by a California pro-gun control organization found students were no safer on campuses in states that had open carry than those in states without it. “The public health research is clear; this is a dangerous, reckless and unnecessary bill,” Bunker said. But Farrington cited a 2025 study from the Crime Prevention Research Center, a conservative, pro-gun advocacy group, that found in the past decade a “good guy” with a gun stopped 36% of reported mass shooting events. “These gun control measures only restrict moral upstanding citizens,” Farrington said. Next Step: The Senate committee has until April 30 to make a recommendation. Outlook: Uncertain. The Senate panel will no doubt alter it in some form, and Gov. Kelly Ayotte has yet to weigh in on it. A hotly debated bill to permit students, staff and visitors to carry weapons on college campuses cleared its first hurdle in the House of Repr… New Hampshire college students turned out both in support and against legislation that would allow students, staff and visitors to legally car… Interim University of New Hampshire Police Chief Steven Lee has been placed on paid administrative leave pending a criminal and internal inves… After the shooting deaths of two Brown University students, House Republicans said Wednesday they'll push to adopt legislation so anyone can legally possess firearms or “non-lethal weapons” on all college campuses in New Hampshire receiving taxpayer support. © Copyright 2026 Union Leader Corp., P.O. content:
Recent regulatory changes lifted Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates for 2027, a key event for UnitedHealth Group (UNH), since this program is a major revenue contributor and directly shapes the company’s earnings outlook. The Medicare Advantage decision and recent analyst upgrades have helped the 1 month share price return reach 11.38%. However, the year to date share price return of 6.60% and 1 year total shareholder return of 44.64% both remain weak. If you are looking beyond a single insurer and want to see how other healthcare names are priced for growth in AI powered care, now is a good time to check out 34 healthcare AI stocks With Medicare Advantage rates reset and analysts turning more constructive, UnitedHealth Group now trades after a 44.64% 1 year total shareholder return decline. Is this weakness an opportunity, or is future growth already priced in? UnitedHealth Group last closed at $314.19, while the most followed narrative on the stock points to a fair value of $486.86, implying a large valuation gap that the market has not closed. Expanding Premium Base: UNH’s premium base continues to grow, contributing to its revenue growth. Contract Wins: The company secures contracts and renewals, such as the recent contract win in Michigan to serve Medicaid beneficiaries. Strength in Optum Business: Optum, a subsidiary of UNH, contributes significantly to its overall performance. Solid Financial Position: UNH’s financial stability reinforces investor confidence. Aging Population: The aging U.S. population sustains demand for UNH’s Medicare plans. Impressive Earnings Surprise History: UNH consistently outperforms earnings estimates. Robust Growth Prospects: The consensus estimates for 2024 and 2025 indicate growth in both earnings and revenues. Strong Return on Equity: UNH efficiently utilizes shareholders’ funds with a higher return on equity than the industry average. Curious what earnings growth path and margin assumptions need to hold up for that valuation gap to make sense, and how long premium and Optum momentum are expected to last. According to WallStreetWontons, the fair value narrative leans heavily on UnitedHealth Group’s scale, its mix of premiums and services, and the contribution from Optum and UnitedHealthcare. That view sits alongside the SWS DCF model, which currently estimates future cash flow value at $816.74 per share, far above the recent $314.19 price. While the narrative highlights revenue growth and stable margins, the DCF output reflects those assumptions compounded over time at a discount rate of 6.06%, which can create a wide gap between market price and model value. title: NH college students protest bill to allow guns on campus - Seacoastonline.com
content: CONCORD — Students are speaking up on the future of gun-free college campuses in New Hampshire, while Gov. Kelly Ayotte has remained silent on the Republican-led bill that would allow students and others on campus to be armed. A protest against New Hampshire House Bill 1793 is being held April 14 outside the State House prior to a New Hampshire Senate Judiciary Committee public hearing on the proposed policy. Proponents of the bill are also expected to attend the hearing. Concealed and open carry are legal in New Hampshire without a license for adults, though all University System of New Hampshire schools are presently gun-free zones. If HB 1793, which passed the House previously, is passed by the state Senate and signed into law by Ayotte, UNH, Keene State, Plymouth State and other college campuses could not regulate firearms. The bill would bar public colleges and universities in New Hampshire from regulating firearm possession across campuses, in addition to non-lethal weapons such as pepper spray, mace, stun guns and tasers. The bill does not contain any provisions requiring safe storage of firearms. It has been opposed by a large majority of University of New Hampshire students, as well as UNH's administration, Durham town leaders and police, all of whom raise safety concerns. State Rep. Sam Farrington, R-Rochester, a current University of New Hampshire student, is the bill’s prime sponsor. Thirteen Republican lawmakers — nine from the House and four from the Senate — have co-sponsored the bill. Proponents like Farrington say they are hearing students want the right to be armed after incidents like the shooting at Brown University in late 2025, saying gun-free school zones don't lead to a violence-free zone. Ayotte's spokesperson had not replied as of mid-day April 14 to a request for comment on the governor's position on the bill. Students from the University of New Hampshire and Plymouth State University, law enforcement officials, UNH residence hall directors and anti-firearm advocates were among the speakers at the rally against HB 1793. Eli Orne, a UNH freshman, and Ronan Sangster, a Plymouth State University junior, represented their campuses at the protest. Orne pointed to mental health challenges in young adults and the risk of increased violence or deaths by suidide posed by HB 1793. “I believe that if people have unrestricted access to firearms, what could usually result in a hospitalization and be managed could become something that is fatal. That person would not be able to go on and live the rest of their lives,” Orne said. Sangster’s father is a career U.S. Marine who “has always ensured that I was familiar with gun safety, gun rights and the catastrophic effects that they can wreak on a human life,” Sangster said. The junior biology major worries if guns are allowed on campus students would not apply for residence hall leadership positions within the university system, increasing the risk for student assaults and mental health incidents to increase and go unreported. Sangster added the bill could lead to decreased enrollment across New Hampshire’s public colleges. “It will decrease educational opportunities for New Hampshire students who cannot afford to go to school out of state, and as such, limit access to housing for those in need. One way or another, this bill is going to kill people,” Sangster said. “It will put everyone involved at unnecessary risk in a place where they're already vulnerable and overall lead to an irreparable degradation of the quality of New Hampshire institutions.” Durham Police Department Deputy Chief Jack Dalton and Strafford County Sheriff Kathryn Mone, a former Durham police sergeant, received cheers for their opposition to HB 1793. Mone stated she received both her bachelor’s and graduate degrees on the UNH Durham campus. “Schools are for learning, not guns,” she said. “I was wearing a gun when I was working, and then I went to the classroom, where I was not wearing a gun. I was depending on the police who were armed and trained to come, if I called … and they were the ones that were going to come to the classroom and handle what needed to be handled, because I was there to learn.” The sheriff shared her belief that policies about firearms on public college campuses should be left to school administrators. According to Dalton, in Durham, 525 arrests have occurred in town since the start of UNH’s academic year. Among those charged, 285 are university students, and 359 arrests were alcohol-related. Guns should not be brought into the setting, Dalton argued. “This is not about rights,” he said. “This is about common sense, because now we’re going to add guns into an equation where we have young adults, 18 to 22 years old for the most part, learning to become young adults, learning to become good citizens. Now they’re going to have guns? We as a police department have sat back and said, ‘Well, how do we handle this?’” At a town hall in Durham in March, Dalton told attendees he would not allow his daughter, a high school senior, to enroll at UNH if HB 1793 becomes law. Farrington recently hosted a question-and-answer session on his bill on UNH’s Durham campus, where he studies. After the event, he said, two students messaged him to report he convinced them to support the measure. The first-term state representative brought forward the bill for “the safety concern and the constitutional right concern,” he stated prior to the April 14 public hearing. “I understand it’s emotional. I understand that some people don’t like guns, they don’t like being around guns. But what it comes down to is that this is a natural right protected by the Constitution,” Farrington said. In response to the Student Senate survey showing vast opposition to HB 1793, Farrington said, “It doesn’t change anything I do. I try to talk to the people that disagree with me.” He reported over 400 college students in New Hampshire signed a petition in support of the bill. “Preventing lawful students from carrying only ensures that potential victims are left defenseless against unlawful aggression,” the bill states. Fourteen states don’t outright ban concealed carry on their public university campuses, according to Everytown, a gun violence prevention organization: Arkansas, Georgia, Idaho, Kansas, Minnesota, Mississippi, Montana, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, West Virginia, Wisconsin and Wyoming. “It worries me because we live in a society where there are deranged people out there and criminals don’t have any regard for morality or what the law says,” Farrington said. “These gun control measures only restrict law-abiding, upstanding citizens.” A National Rifle Association lobbyist and state representative of the Oklahoma-based Women for Gun Rights were among the advocates of Farrington’s bill in Concord before the hearing. Farrington briefly congregated with younger pro-firearm advocates on the steps of the State House. “It’s a broad coalition,” he said of HB 1793’s supporters. “This bill seeks to allow anyone to carry a loaded firearm at any time, anywhere on campus,” said Zandra Rice Hawkins, director of GunSense NH and executive director of Engage NH. “We're talking not just classrooms, but dorms, large sporting venues, childcare centers that are on campus, contract events and more. This poses a significant danger to students, staff, visitors, to youth groups coming in the summer or on the weekends for youth tournaments or camps. This is not the kind of legislation we want to see in our state.” The brief rally was supported by numerous organizations: GunSense NH, Engage NH, NH Youth Movement, Moms Demand Action, MomsRising and the National Campaign to Keep Guns Off Campus. Thirty-eight seats and minimal standing room was available in the chamber ahead of the Senate Judiciary Committee hearing. Dozens of people began to sign up to testify on the bill roughly an hour before it began. Tim Hoheneder, a University of New Hampshire doctorate candidate in Durham, is the college’s student representative on the University System of New Hampshire’s board of trustees. Hoheneder’s peers were among those planning to testify before the Senate subcommittee and he appeared at the protest. He said UNH students taking the time to travel from Durham to Concord for the protest and hearing "really means a lot, not only to those of us who want to keep our campuses safe, but also to the institutional mission and institutional autonomy that USNH wants to keep over its campuses, as well." This story will be updated with more coverage from the events outside the State House. The use and possession of firearms is banned across the University of New Hampshire’s three campuses in Durham, Concord and Manchester. Written permission for “instructional or other qualified purposes and in other special circumstances and conditions” may be issued by the university’s police chief, per school policy. The university system reported its expenses in response to the bill could fall between $100,000 and $500,000 across its colleges, according to the bill’s fiscal note. Opponents of the bill have warned that could lead to further tuition hikes at USNH schools, which increased in-state tuition this academic year for the first time in six years. Schools within the university system could face lawsuits requesting a minimum of $10,000 in damages if HB 1793 becomes law. “Any individual aggrieved by a violation of this subdivision may bring an action against the public institution of higher education and its employees responsible for the violation and seek appropriate relief, including injunctive relief, monetary damages, reasonable attorneys' fees, and court costs,” the bill says. “Total damages awarded in an action brought under this subdivision shall be at least $10,000.” Daniel Gray, executive director of Citizens Alliance of New Hampshire, warned supporters of HB 1793 in an April 9 email that state senators may introduce amendments that would "significantly weaken” the bill. Gray called for the bill to be advanced as drafted. “This is our chance to expand our Second Amendment rights and give college students and faculty the ability to protect and defend themselves,” Gray’s message states. The University of New Hampshire’s Student Senate organized a survey regarding HB 1793, receiving 2,361 respondents from the Durham campus. Among them, 81.8% of students “strongly oppose” the bill, while another 3.5% are “somewhat opposed” to HB 1793, according to the Student Senate’s finalized report. In addition, 10.8% of student respondents strongly support HB 1793. Community town halls regarding the bill and its expected impacts have been held in Durham, Keene and Plymouth, home of the public schools within the University of New Hampshire System. House legislators advanced HB 1793 to the Republican-majority New Hampshire Senate in February after passing the bill in a 188-165 vote. The bill is now under review by the Senate Judiciary Committee. The policy would take effect Jan. 1, 2027, if signed into law.

title: Bill to end gun restrictions at NH colleges comes in for criticism at public hearing - New Hampshire Public Radio
content: A bill to bar state colleges and universities from curbing gun rights on their campuses drew a crowd to Concord Tuesday. Most came to fight the bill, including the president of University of New Hampshire, students from several state colleges, and multiple members of law enforcement. “We are stunned we are even here today talking about this,” Durham Deputy Police Chief Jack Dalton said at a small rally before the bill’s state Senate hearing. “Hopefully common sense prevails, so we can move on in Durham.” Under the bill, anyone who is legally allowed to carry a weapon could do so on a college campus in the state, without limit. The proposal is similar but more sweeping than laws already on the books in about a dozen states. Its backers say the bill, which cleared the New Hampshire House along party lines in February, will make campuses safer while honoring fundamental rights. “We want senators to understand, they took an oath to defend the Constitution,” said Rep. Sam Farrington of Rochester, the bill’s lead sponsor and a current UNH student. “College students are adults and deserving of all their rights.” The bill would block any college or university that accepts taxpayer funding of any kind from regulating firearms, as well as non-lethal weapons like pepper spray, stun guns or tasers. Right now, UNH students are allowed to keep guns for hunting or target shooting but must store them at the local police department. This bill would allow students to store weapons in their dorm rooms. According to several people who showed up for the hearing who manage college dormitories, the atmosphere there is already volatile enough. “A lot of our incidents revolve around alcohol,” said Hans Hendricks, who has been a UNH resident hall director for three years. “We see it every single week, and truly I cannot say it enough: We don’t need guns added to the mix.” That message was echoed by top college administrators, who stressed that this bill goes farther than similar policies in effect in other states. This would make New Hampshire unique, said UNH president Elizabeth Chilton: “It would be the most liberal or the most conservative, depending on the way that you look at it.” According to Farrington, that was by design: “This would be the best campus carry statute in the entire nation,” he told lawmakers. Read more: ‘Campus carry’ bill has NH college students divided: Do guns mean freedom or fear? But the proposal’s breadth appeared to be a sticking point with several senators. “I am a big gun person, but I do have concerns,” Republican Sen. Bill Gannon of Sandown said, noting that even U.S. military academies bar cadets from keeping guns. “I am scared that institutions that really know weapons have chosen not to have it in their dorms.” Republican. Sen Daryl Abbas, meanwhile, pushed Farrington about whether his bill should include provisions to allow colleges and universities to punish students from carrying weapons while drunk. “It seems to me pretty much a no-brainer,” Abbas said after Farrington at first seemed to oppose the idea. “I would support an amendment, so long as it was carefully written, that allows the school to regulate that,” Farrington said. Winning favor with the Senate Judiciary Committee, which is expected to vote on it later this month, will be this bill’s first test. Republicans hold a 16-8 Senate majority, and this bill has four Senate co-sponsors. But Gov. Kelly Ayotte, who has prioritized policies she says promote public safety, has yet to take a position on the bill. Read more: Students ask governor and lawmakers to make NH more affordable so they can stay Tuesday’s hearing attracted several dozen students to the State House plaza who held signs protesting the proposed bill. Emily Hunt, a student at the University of New Hampshire, said she felt safe on campus but was worried that making it easier for people to carry guns would change that dynamic. “I spent all of my education, K through 12, being afraid of a school shooting, both for myself and for my loved ones,” Hunt said. “And I don't want that at college.” Eli Orne, a UNH freshman, said he was concerned that more guns on campus could lead to more suicides as it would be easier for students in crisis to get their hands on a deadly weapon. “Because when you have access to a gun in any capacity — it doesn't have to be yours, it could just be around — your risk of dying by suicide increases,” he said.
